current_fact.fact_text: In 1961, the integrated circuit patent given to Robert Noyce converged with Jack Kilby’s earlier work, sparking what became the microelectronics revolution. That moment turned the idea of a 'computer' from something filling a room into something that could, one day, fit in a pocket — or inside a studio rack, shaping music in ways no one yet imagined.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

current_fact.match_reason: Microchips made portable music players and digital listening possible; this fact pairs with an episode that critically reflects on how those very devices changed our relationship to sound.

queue.fact_text: On April 25, 1945, as diplomats designed institutions and partisans liberated cities, many artists and civilians were simply imagining what life after war might feel like. The converging events of that single day — uprisings, conferences, encounters on the Elbe — marked a global pivot from total war toward the fragile, imperfect experiment of peace that continues to this day.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: A day that tilted the world from war toward the possibility of peace finds a gentle echo in an episode about slow summers, healing rhythms, and returning to a quieter, more human pace.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1983, NASA's Pioneer 10 became the first human-made object to travel beyond the orbit of Pluto, silently continuing its journey into interstellar space after already giving us our first close-up views of Jupiter. Long after its power faded, the spacecraft still carries a golden plaque engraved with a map to our Sun and silhouettes of humans — a quiet cosmic postcard from Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A story about a spacecraft pushing into the emptiness beyond Pluto fits an episode that reimagines emptiness as creative space and carries a cosmic, forward-motion vibe.

published.fact_text: In 1960, the USS Triton completed the first fully submerged circumnavigation of the globe, staying underwater for 60 days as part of a secret Cold War mission called Operation Sandblast. The crew navigated by inertial guidance and periscope sightings, effectively turning the submarine into a stealthy planet-hugging satellite beneath the waves.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A submarine circling the Earth underwater echoes the episode’s themes of immersion, suspension, and moving fluidly beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1983, ten-year-old Samantha Smith received an extraordinary reply from Soviet leader Yuri Andropov, inviting her to visit the USSR after she wrote him a letter about her fear of nuclear war. Her journey turned a child's curiosity into a small but powerful act of citizen diplomacy at the height of the Cold War.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: Samantha Smith’s child-led peace gesture resonates with an episode centered on political voice, resistance, and individual courage across borders.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 2015, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck Nepal near Gorkha, triggering avalanches on Mount Everest and devastating centuries-old temples in Kathmandu. The disaster not only reshaped the landscape but also revealed the resilience of communities rebuilding among the ruins of their cultural heritage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A profound natural disaster and long recovery parallel an episode about healing after sudden rupture, with reflective, cinematic music echoing resilience.

published.fact_text: In 1792, French army officer Claude Joseph Rouget de Lisle composed “La Marseillaise” in a single night as a battle song for troops defending revolutionary France. Its fierce melody and lyrics were so powerful that the song was banned and revived multiple times before becoming the French national anthem.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

published.match_reason: A revolutionary anthem from France pairs with an episode featuring theatrical French pop and songs that, like anthems, ignite collective emotion on the dancefloor.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1981, more than 100 workers at Japan's Tsuruga Nuclear Power Plant were exposed to radiation during repairs, in an incident that later helped fuel public distrust of nuclear safety. The plant, sitting on a narrow peninsula, became a symbol of the uneasy balance between high-risk energy and densely populated coasts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A nuclear radiation incident connects metaphorically to the idea of raw electrical power and 'shock,' contrasted here by channeling energy safely through electrifying rock instead of reactors.

published.fact_text: In 1953, James Watson and Francis Crick published their paper "Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids," proposing the double-helix structure of DNA. That elegant twisted ladder explained how life encodes information and replicates itself, turning an invisible molecule into one of the most iconic images in science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The decoding of DNA is a triumph of scientific curiosity and pattern-finding, aligning with an episode about how learning reshapes the brain and the joy that comes from understanding complex structures.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1945, American and Soviet troops met on the River Elbe near Torgau, effectively cutting Nazi Germany in two. The spontaneous celebration between soldiers who didn’t share a language but shared relief became known as Elbe Day, a fleeting moment of unity in a fractured world.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The dramatic turning point toward the end of a war matches the raw, foundational energy of Sedna’s very first episode, a symbolic 'beginning' after destruction.

published.fact_text: In 1954, Bell Telephone Laboratories unveiled the first practical silicon solar cell, demonstrating that sunlight could be directly converted into electricity with unprecedented efficiency. At just a few centimeters across, these tiny wafers quietly kicked off the age of solar power and the dream of harvesting starlight for energy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Solar cells turning sunlight into calm, steady power reflect an episode about slower planetary rhythms and gentle, sun-drenched downtempo moods.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1980, Dan-Air Flight 1008 crashed into a mountain near Tenerife after a navigation error, killing all 146 people on board. The tragedy led to changes in air-traffic procedures and highlighted how complex radio instructions and human factors can ripple into disaster in the thin air above volcanic islands.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A tragic aviation accident over dramatic landscapes pairs with a contemplative episode about fragile journeys in extreme environments, using cinematic music to hold heavy stories softly.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1959, the St. Lawrence Seaway officially opened, connecting the Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean via a chain of locks, canals, and channels. It effectively gave ocean-going ships a 3,700-kilometer freshwater highway into the heart of North America, reshaping trade and the ecology of the lakes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A grand waterway turning inland seas into maritime routes aligns with an episode built around boat journeys and coastal music, both celebrating travel across connected waters.

published.fact_text: In 1882, French commandant Henri Rivière seized the citadel of Hanoi with a relatively small force of marine infantry, provoking a spiral of conflict in Tonkin. What began as a daring colonial gamble on April 25 soon contributed to decades of resistance and reshaped Vietnam’s relationship with European powers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: French colonial actions in Asia mirror, in another region, the themes of occupation, exile, and resistance explored through Tuareg desert blues.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1829, British naval officer Charles Fremantle arrived off the coast of what is now Western Australia aboard HMS Challenger, soon declaring the Swan River Colony for the British Empire. The proclamation overlooked tens of thousands of years of Aboriginal presence, marking yet another moment where a few lines of speech rewrote maps for entire peoples.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An imperial ship arriving to claim distant shores connects with an episode recorded on a boat, reframing seafaring from conquest into shared, reflective travel.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 2014, officials in Flint, Michigan switched the city’s water source to the Flint River as a cost-saving measure, inadvertently exposing residents to lead and bacterial contamination. The crisis, driven by corroding pipes and ignored warnings, turned drinking water into a symbol of environmental injustice and institutional failure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A story about poisoned infrastructure and broken trust parallels an episode questioning our dependence on invisible digital systems, both urging us to re-examine what we take for granted in everyday life.

published.fact_text: In 2005, the final piece of the ancient Obelisk of Axum was returned to Ethiopia after having been taken to Italy by Mussolini’s forces in 1937. The 24-meter monolith, once a symbol of imperial plunder, became a powerful example of cultural restitution and the slow journey home of stolen heritage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: The repatriation of a stolen Ethiopian monument aligns beautifully with a dub/reggae selection that explicitly includes 'Ethiopia' and themes of Babylon, exile, and spiritual return.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1982, Israel completed its withdrawal from the Sinai Peninsula, fulfilling a core condition of the Camp David Accords with Egypt. A landscape of sand and oil fields became the backdrop for one of the 20th century’s rare cases where long-term territorial conflict gave way—however imperfectly—to a negotiated peace.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A carefully negotiated withdrawal from contested land fits an episode about travel, shifting perspective, and the quiet inner changes that make peace possible.

published.fact_text: In 1961, engineer Robert Noyce was granted a patent for a monolithic integrated circuit built on silicon, a key step toward the microchips inside nearly every modern device. By etching entire circuits onto a single wafer, Noyce helped shrink rooms filled with electronics into chips smaller than a fingernail.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: The integrated circuit is the hardware foundation that made modern digital audio and tools like Auto-Tune possible, so a microchip patent naturally pairs with an episode about the technology transforming sound.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1945, Italy’s National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy called for a general uprising against Nazi occupation and the Italian Social Republic, a date now commemorated as Liberation Day. Partisans, workers, and civilians helped topple a regime, turning city streets into corridors of resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: Italy’s Liberation Day uprising resonates strongly with an episode about Rage Against the Machine, whose music is steeped in anti-fascist, anti-authoritarian activism and street-level protest.

published.fact_text: Also on April 25, 1945, delegates from 50 nations gathered in San Francisco for the United Nations Conference on International Organization, drafting the UN Charter. After years of global war, they attempted to bottle the fragile hope of collective security into the architecture of a new world body.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: The birth of a global institution uniting many nations pairs with a 'collective voyage through sound' that blends genres and geographies into a shared destination.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1898, the United States Congress declared that a state of war with Spain had existed since April 21, retroactively legitimizing the naval blockade of Cuba. The Spanish–American War, short but transformative, marked a turning point where the U.S. stepped fully onto the stage as an overseas imperial power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A war that reshaped Spain’s global role pairs with an episode that centers Spanish cultural identity through the deep, emotional history of flamenco.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 1960, the same submarine USS Triton that circled the globe also proved how effectively nuclear reactors could power vessels for months without refueling. Nuclear submarines turned the deep ocean into an invisible strategic theatre, where endurance and silence mattered more than speed.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: A long, continuous underwater journey powered by hidden reactors mirrors an unbroken, slowly unfolding musical flow designed for drifting beyond the visible surface of the day.

published.fact_text: On April 25, 2015, the Nepal earthquake not only shifted tectonic plates but also altered Everest’s height by a small amount, reminding us that the world’s tallest peak is not a fixed monument but a breathing part of Earth’s crust. In a few seconds, villages, glaciers, and pilgrimage routes were subtly rearranged on a planetary scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A seismic event that reshapes mountains fits an episode about weathering storms and trusting that even the most violent transitions eventually give way to clearer skies.

published.fact_text: In 1961, the integrated circuit patent given to Robert Noyce converged with Jack Kilby’s earlier work, sparking what became the microelectronics revolution. That moment turned the idea of a 'computer' from something filling a room into something that could, one day, fit in a pocket — or inside a studio rack, shaping music in ways no one yet imagined.

In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod.
